nativeRange.habitat: Rocky deepwater zones of Lake Tanganyika, typically found in the northern portion of the lake at depth over rocky/sandy substrates

decorPreferences: Large cave formations or overhanging rockwork
Open swimming areas between rock structures

diet.notes: Primarily accepts frozen and prepared meaty foods — krill, mysis shrimp, silversides, prawns, and earthworms are commonly recommended. High-quality sinking cichlid pellets are generally well-accepted. Feeder fish and mammalian/avian meat products are best avoided to reduce disease risk and inappropriate fat content.

goodWith: Other large Tanganyikan cichlids (Altolamprologus, Julidochromis, Lepidiolamprologus)
Larger Tropheus species (in sufficiently large tanks)
avoidWith: Small fish that fit in its mouth (predatory of small fish)
Mbuna-type cichlids (incompatible water chemistry preferences and aggression mismatch)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Hectonichus, 2505x1881. Sharp full-lateral view showing the classic bold black/blue-and-white vertical bars, prominent nuchal hump and vivid blue fins against orange rock; most diagnostic and striking.
Photo by Patrick Ch. Apfeld, 4928x3264. Tack-sharp, intensely blue Zaire-type individual with clear nuchal hump; high resolution and vibrant colour.
Photo by Ethmostigmus, 5142x3428. Group of frontosa under blue lighting showing the shoaling/harem social nature, bold bars and hump; front fish crisp.

summary: The Frontosa is a large, long-lived deepwater cichlid from Lake Tanganyika, recognized by its distinctive nuchal hump and bold blue-and-white striped pattern. Best kept as a harem group in a spacious, hard, alkaline aquarium, it tends to be slow-moving and relatively calm for its size.
careNotes:
The Frontosa (Cyphotilapia frontosa) is endemic to Lake Tanganyika and requires hard, alkaline rift-lake water chemistry — untreated soft tap water is generally unsuitable without buffering. In the wild this is a deepwater fish, typically found at 15–70 m and recorded as deep as 120 m, and it tends to prefer subdued lighting and a calm, unhurried aquarium environment.

A minimum group of five is broadly recommended, with eight to twelve being closer to what many experienced keepers suggest for stable social dynamics. The classic arrangement is one dominant male kept with multiple females — a structure commonly called a harem group.

Several geographic color variants are recognized in the trade: 'Burundi' (the classic six-stripe blue-and-white form), 'Mpimbwe' (blue stripes on a white or gray body, from southern Tanzania), 'Zaire Blue' or 'Zaire' (with more extensive blue coloration), and 'Kigoma' (with broader, darker bars). A captive-bred blue-fluorescent strain has also emerged in the hobby. These variants are sometimes sold under their location names and tend to command a price premium.

The Frontosa is predatory toward fish small enough to swallow — small tetras, danios, and juvenile cichlids are all at risk in the same tank. It is NOT suitable for a general community aquarium. Its large adult size (commonly 30–35 cm in aquaria, with wild specimens reportedly reaching around 40 cm) and group requirements call for a substantial tank: a minimum of 180 cm in length and 500 liters for a proper harem.

The Frontosa is notably long-lived — 15–25 years in well-maintained aquaria is commonly reported — making it a significant long-term commitment. Growth is slow, and juveniles may take several years to develop the characteristic nuchal hump (the rounded, fatty crest on top of the head) that gives the species much of its distinctive look. The hump tends to be more pronounced in males.
breedingNotes: The Frontosa is a maternal mouthbrooder, meaning the female incubates eggs and fry inside her mouth (buccal cavity) rather than guarding a nest. Clutch sizes are relatively small by cichlid standards — typically 10 to 50 eggs. Females also practice what is sometimes called buccal feeding, continuing to pass food to the fry while still holding them. Breeding in captivity is considered moderately challenging; a well-conditioned harem group kept in a stable, mature aquarium is the approach most commonly recommended. Patience is important, as spawning may take some time to establish even in ideal conditions. A ratio of one male to four or six females tends to be cited as a productive group structure.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of Aquarium Co-Op, which has no dedicated Frontosa care page. SeriouslyFish provides the most comprehensive species-specific data for this taxon.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (30–35 cm), temperature (22–28°C), pH (8.0–9.0), tank size, and lifespan (15–25 years). Minor disagreement on minimum tank size (125 gal / ~473 L vs SeriouslyFish's 864 L for a group); biased toward the larger SeriouslyFish recommendation given the group-living and predatory size requirements. Breeding difficulty listed as 'easy' vs SeriouslyFish 'moderately challenging'; resolved to 'moderate' consistent with user-provided key data.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy cross-check: confirms Cichlidae, Cichliformes (modern classification). Max TL 33 cm consistent with captive range. IUCN Near Threatened status noted. Hardness range 8–12 dH on FishBase is narrower than hobby sources; hobby sources used for aquarium parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder with a buccal incubation period of 35–50 days and a typical clutch of 10–50 eggs. Reliable spawning generally requires an established harem group and consistently stable water conditions.
